message:
chore(beads): close br-r37-c1-lnqss (generic_bfs_edges default-neighbors)

Fixed in commit 8f2086d.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -75,7 +75,7 @@
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-lby4x","title":"parity: isolates() returns list_iterator, nx returns generator (eager vs lazy evaluation)","description":"fnx.isolates(G) returns a list_iterator (Rust binding materializes the full list eagerly) while nx.isolates(G) returns a generator (lazy). Behavioral drift: code doing isinstance(result, types.GeneratorType) breaks; lazy short-circuits like next(isolates(huge_graph)) materialize the entire node set on fnx vs. one node on nx. Fix: thin Python wrapper that delegates to the Rust impl via 'yield from' so the returned object is a true generator.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T05:44:38.565317140Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T05:46:20.491389089Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T05:46:20.489589242Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit ea4a271. fnx.isolates(G) now returns a true generator matching nx's contract. 8 parity tests freeze the contract.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-lim0x","title":"eulerian_circuit edge order/traversal direction drifts from nx","description":"fnx.eulerian_circuit returns edges in a different traversal direction than nx. nx walks the circuit in reverse vs. fnx's direction.

Repro:
  edges = [('a','b'),('b','c'),('c','a'),('a','d'),('d','e'),('e','a')]
  fnx -> [('a','b'),('b','c'),('c','a'),('a','d'),('d','e'),('e','a')]
  nx  -> [('a','e'),('e','d'),('d','a'),('a','c'),('c','b'),('b','a')]

  edges = [(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,0)]
  fnx -> [(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,0)]
  nx  -> [(0,3),(3,2),(2,1),(1,0)]

Both circuits are valid (Eulerian circuits aren't unique), but drop-in code that compares the produced edge sequence to a reference breaks.

Fix: delegate eulerian_circuit (and likely eulerian_path) to nx for parity.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T13:32:18.379695234Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T13:35:17.333267853Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T13:35:17.331312850Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-lmviu","title":"parity: add_path/add_cycle/add_star keyword parameter names","description":"NetworkX exposes add_path(G_to_add_to, nodes_for_path, **attr), add_cycle(G_to_add_to, nodes_for_cycle, **attr), and add_star(G_to_add_to, nodes_for_star, **attr). FNX currently names these parameters G and nodes, so documented keyword calls like fnx.add_path(G_to_add_to=G, nodes_for_path=[1,2]) raise TypeError. Align the public signatures while preserving positional behavior and add parity tests.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cod-networkx","created_at":"2026-04-25T06:20:06.172080189Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T06:23:19.168859030Z","closed_at":"2026-04-25T06:23:19.167009410Z","close_reason":"Completed in 92bae80","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"br-r37-c1-lnqss","title":"generic_bfs_edges (Rust default-neighbors path) yields edges in wrong order","description":"fnx.generic_bfs_edges has two paths: a Python branch (when neighbors=callback is provided) that matches nx, and a Rust _fnx.generic_bfs_edges_rust branch (default neighbors=None) that yields edges in reversed adj order.

Repro:
  edges = [(c,d),(a,b),(b,c),(d,e),(a,c)]
  Both libs: adj[a] = [b, c]

  fnx.generic_bfs_edges(g, source=a) -> [(a,c),(a,b),(c,d),(d,e)]
  nx.generic_bfs_edges(gx, source=a) -> [(a,b),(a,c),(c,d),(d,e)]

  fnx.generic_bfs_edges(g, source=a, neighbors=g.neighbors) -> [(a,b),(a,c),...] (matches nx)

The plain bfs_edges variant matches nx; only generic_bfs_edges via the Rust default-neighbors path drifts.

Fix: route through the Python path always, defaulting neighbors to G.neighbors when None. The Python branch already matches nx exactly. Also remove the dead "if neighbors is None: neighbors = G.neighbors" line inside the "if neighbors is not None" block.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T19:01:23.195765293Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T19:02:14.745680611Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"br-r37-c1-lnqss","title":"generic_bfs_edges (Rust default-neighbors path) yields edges in wrong order","description":"fnx.generic_bfs_edges has two paths: a Python branch (when neighbors=callback is provided) that matches nx, and a Rust _fnx.generic_bfs_edges_rust branch (default neighbors=None) that yields edges in reversed adj order.

Repro:
  edges = [(c,d),(a,b),(b,c),(d,e),(a,c)]
  Both libs: adj[a] = [b, c]

  fnx.generic_bfs_edges(g, source=a) -> [(a,c),(a,b),(c,d),(d,e)]
  nx.generic_bfs_edges(gx, source=a) -> [(a,b),(a,c),(c,d),(d,e)]

  fnx.generic_bfs_edges(g, source=a, neighbors=g.neighbors) -> [(a,b),(a,c),...] (matches nx)

The plain bfs_edges variant matches nx; only generic_bfs_edges via the Rust default-neighbors path drifts.

Fix: route through the Python path always, defaulting neighbors to G.neighbors when None. The Python branch already matches nx exactly. Also remove the dead "if neighbors is None: neighbors = G.neighbors" line inside the "if neighbors is not None" block.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T19:01:23.195765293Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T19:03:53.615994642Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T19:03:53.613905654Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-lz7u6","title":"parity: connectivity bridge_components missing","description":"networkx.algorithms.connectivity.bridge_components(G) returns 2-edge-connected components after removing bridges. Not exposed by franken_networkx. Add Python wrapper delegating to nx.algorithms.connectivity.bridge_components via _networkx_graph_for_parity, export at top-level and add to __all__.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-25T11:44:53.163889437Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T03:14:43.967902315Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T03:14:43.966070217Z","close_reason":"Already implemented in fnx — top-level export matches nx.connectivity exactly. Landed tests/python/test_connectivity_parity_extras.py (commit 3765516) with 37 cases freezing the contract.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-m4msi","title":"parity: utils.random_sequence powerlaw_sequence + zipf_rv + cumulative_distribution + is_valid_tree_degree_sequence (4 functions)","description":"Four random-sequence utilities from networkx.utils.random_sequence not surfaced by franken_networkx: powerlaw_sequence(n, exponent=2.0, seed=None), zipf_rv(alpha, xmin=1, seed=None), cumulative_distribution(distribution), is_valid_tree_degree_sequence(degree_sequence). These are used by stochastic graph generators and degree-sequence sanity checks. Add Python wrappers in __init__.py exporting at top-level and adding to __all__.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-25T21:59:39.857059179Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T03:18:46.320103675Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T03:18:46.318235318Z","close_reason":"Already implemented. powerlaw_sequence/zipf_rv/cumulative_distribution exposed at fnx top level matching nx.utils bit-for-bit when seeded; discrete_sequence on fnx.utils; is_valid_tree_degree_sequence is fnx-only ((bool, reason) tuple). Landed tests/python/test_random_sequence_parity.py (commit d2ed994) with 89 cases freezing the contract.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-r37-c1-mnrem","title":"parity: fnx.approximation.* exposed (G, *args, **kwargs) catch-all signatures, hiding all real kwargs","description":"_ApproximationNamespace dynamically wrapped each approximation function as (G, *args, **kwargs), so inspect.signature(fnx.approximation.X) returned a useless catch-all for every function. nx exposes the documented per-function signatures (average_clustering(G, trials, seed), christofides(G, weight, tree), greedy_tsp(G, weight, source), etc). 27 functions affected. Drop-in callers couldn't see what kwargs are accepted via introspection, IDEs and type-checkers couldn't help, and typos passed silently into nx's **kwargs handling. Fix: functools.wraps(nx_func) on the wrapper so the original signature/name/docstring are preserved. 30 parity tests freeze the contract.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T04:41:51.191871760Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T04:42:37.315185389Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T04:42:37.313339806Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit 63a0163. _ApproximationNamespace.__getattr__ now uses functools.wraps so every fnx.approximation.X carries nx's exact signature, name, and docstring. 30 parity tests freeze the contract; all 27 previously-mismatched functions now pass introspection.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
